MKT10009 Marketing and the Consumer ExperienceLecture 4 - Customer-driven market strategy: Creating value for target customers. Part 1  - Customer-driven marketing strategy and Market segmentation.
Chapter 6 from our Textbook: Principles of Marketing,
8th edition
Presented by Dr Gordon Campbell

Learning Objectives for Lecture 4
6.1 Define the main steps in designing a customer-driven marketing strategy: market segmentation, targeting, differentiation and positioning.
6.2 List and discuss the main bases for segmenting consumer and business markets.
6.3 Explain how companies identify attractive market segments and choose a market-targeting strategy.
6.4 Discuss how companies differentiate and position their products for maximum competitive advantage.

Part 1  - Customer-driven marketing strategy and Market segmentation.

So, what is marketing? Customer-driven market strategy
Figure 1.6:
An expanded model of the marketing process

Customer-driven marketing strategy (1 of 2)
Most companies have moved away from mass marketing and towards target marketing.
Companies must design customer-driven marketing strategies that build the right relationships with the right customers.

Figure 6.1
Designing a customer-driven marketing strategy – the four steps:
Segmentation > Targeting > Differentiation > Positioning

Market segmentation (1 of 8)
Buyers in any market differ in their wants, resources, locations, buying attitudes and buying practices.
Through market segmentation, companies more efficiently and effectively match their unique needs with products and services.
There is no single way to segment a market.

Market segmentation (2 of 8)
Segmenting consumer markets
There are four different types of segmentation.
Geographic segmentation refers to dividing a market into different geographical units, such as nations, regions, states, local government areas, cities or neighbourhoods.
Demographic segmentation refers to dividing a market into segments based on variables such as age, gender, family size, family life cycle, income, occupation, education, religion, race, generation and nationality.

Market segmentation (3 of 8)
Segmenting consumer markets (cont.)
Psychographic segmentation refers to dividing a market into different segments based on social class, lifestyle or personality characteristics.
Behavioural segmentation refers to dividing a market into segments based on consumer knowledge, attitudes, uses or responses to a product.

Table 6.1
Bases for identifying and analysing consumer market segments (selected examples)
Variable
Typical breakdowns
Geographic
Region
Far North Queensland, Western NSW, Lakes District – Victoria, …
City size (population)
Under 5000; 5000–20 000; …; 2 000 000 and over
Demographic
Age (years)
Under 6, 7–11, 12–19, 20–34, ..., 65+
Sex
Male, female
Family size (members)
1–2, 3–4, 5+
Psychographic
Socioeconomic
Quintiles
Personality
Compulsive, gregarious, authoritarian, ambitious
Behavioural
Purchase occasion
Regular occasion, special occasion
Benefits sought
Quality, service, economy
User status
Non-user, ex-user, potential user, first-time user, regular user

Market segmentation (5 of 8)
Segmenting consumer markets (cont.)
Using multiple segmentation bases
Marketers rarely limit their segmentation analysis to only one or a few variables.
Q: Why would marketers use multiple segmentation bases? When might they use only one or a few variables?

Market segmentation (6 of 8)
Segmenting business markets
Business marketers use many of the same variables as consumer markets to segment their markets.
Business marketers also use some additional variables.
Q: Name some of the additional variables (compared to consumer markets) that business marketers use to segment their markets.

Market segmentation (7 of 8)
Segmenting international markets
Companies can segment international markets using one or a combination of several variables, including:
geographic location
economic factors
Stage of market development
political and legal factors
cultural factors
Competitive status of the brand – e.g. Leader (dominant), challenger, follower, niche.
Marketers also use intermarket segmentation – forming segments of consumers who have similar needs and buying behaviour even though they are located in different countries.

Market segmentation (8 of 8)
Requirements for effective segmentation
There are many ways to segment a market, but not all segmentations are effective.
To be useful, market segments must be:
measurable
accessible
substantial
differentiable
actionable.

Market segmentation
A: Use multiple bases to gain further insights into consumers and markets.
To identify different segments and needs.
To segment for different stages in the marketing process;
for example, for Marketing communications planning you might segment using factors such as brand awareness, attitude towards the brand, readiness to buy stage.
For product planning you would be likely to segment by factors to do with unmet consumer needs.
Use one or a few bases when the consumers are mostly homogeneous – mostly have same or similar needs.
Q: Why would marketers use multiple segmentation bases? When might they use only one or a few variables?

Market segmentation
A:
Type of technology used in the customer’s process/es (because they will be likely to need different products compared with customers using a different process).
Size of customer’s business – different size businesses will have different needs and will warrant different levels of sales and service support.
Available/Not available – some customers will be tied to another supplier by ownership* or family ties. (*for example – they are owned by a competitor, or they and a competitor share a common owner). Others will be free to buy from us if they wish to do so.
Q: Name some of the additional variables (compared to consumer markets) that business marketers use to segment their markets.

Coming up next
Part 2 - Market targeting.
